ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general deer meat food product class; no specific stir-fried black pepper venison entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers venison/deer meat as a category; no specific stir-fried preparation code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226955003 covers venison as a substance; no stir-fried preparation concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of venison stir-fried with black pepper is approximately 250 g, representing a shared Zi Char dish portion for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Venison, raw (USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 174299) adapted for stir-fried preparation with black pepper sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central provides raw venison (deer meat) nutrient data (FDC ID 174299). Values have been adapted for stir-fried preparation: fat increased by approximately 3–4 g per 100 g to account for cooking oil absorption; sodium increased to reflect soy sauce and oyster sauce seasoning typical of Malaysian/Singaporean Zi Char black pepper stir-fry. Carbohydrate adjusted upward slightly for sauce ingredients (oyster sauce, soy sauce). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA consulted for regional preparation context; no exact match found for this specific dish in those databases.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — meat and protein-dominant mixed dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for venison stir-fried with black pepper. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous protein-dominant stir-fried meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. Protein-dominant meat dishes consistently show GI ≤ 20 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021). The sauce components (oyster sauce, soy sauce) contribute minimal glycemic carbohydrate. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8.75) / 100 ≈ 1.3, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 3.5 × 2.5 = 8.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1300 mg per restaurant serving), which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake and elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions, as its low GI (estimated 15), high protein content (22.5 g per 100 g), and minimal carbohydrate (3.5 g per 100 g) support stable postprandial blood glucose; however, sodium from the sauce should be monitored if hypertension co-exists.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish with caution due to its elevated sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g) and moderate saturated fat (2.1 g per 100 g); venison is leaner than many red meats, which is a relative advantage, but the sauce preparation adds sodium and fat that increase cardiovascular risk with frequent consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Venison Stir-Fried with Black Pepper is a Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char dish featuring thinly sliced or cubed deer meat (venison) wok-fried at high heat with freshly cracked black pepper, garlic, onion, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, often finished with spring onion. The dish delivers a bold, peppery, savoury flavour profile with a slightly smoky wok-hei aroma. Venison is a lean game meat with notably high protein content (approximately 22.5 g per 100 g) and lower fat than beef or pork, making it a nutritionally favourable red meat choice. The black pepper sauce contributes significant sodium (approximately 520 mg per 100 g), which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is very low (estimated 15), making this dish appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. Iron content is high (approximately 3.8 mg per 100 g), supporting haematological health. The dish is typically served as a shared plate in Zi Char restaurants across Malaysia and Singapore, priced at the premium end of the Zi Char menu due to the relative scarcity of venison. It is not considered halal by default as the halal status of venison sourcing must be verified. Common allergens include soy and shellfish derivatives from oyster sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in black pepper venison?
Is deer meat good for diabetics?
What is the protein content of venison?
Is black pepper venison high in sodium?
Can I eat venison stir-fry if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact match for venison stir-fried with black pepper was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central raw venison data (FDC ID 174299) with adjustments for stir-fried preparation and sauce ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for venison and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this preparation. Nutrient values represent an adapted estimate for the stir-fried dish as served in Malaysian/Singaporean Zi Char restaurants; actual values may vary by restaurant, portion size, and sauce recipe. The halal status is marked false as venison sourcing and slaughter method must be independently verified for halal certification. The dish is tagged with 'Crab' and 'Zi Char' cuisine tags in the source field survey data, which reflects the restaurant category rather than the dish ingredients; these tags are preserved in context only. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous protein-dominant stir-fried meat dishes; this is a low-GI food by all classification systems. GL per 250 g serving = 1.3. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central raw venison data adapted for cooked preparation.
